coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cu2+ with carbon dots chelated Eu-based metal-organic frameworks
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Juan
Liu, Fenfen
Liu, Nan
Zeng, Mulang
Song, Yonghai
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Here, a novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)@Eu-2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) metal organic frameworks (MOFs) was synthesized by encapsulating CDs into the Eu-DPA MOFs for the detection of Cu2+. The as-prepared CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs fluorescent probe was investigated by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results showed the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs exhibited the uniform ball-flower-like nanostructures and were very stable in aqueous solution due to their nanoscaled size and the ball-flower-like nanostructures. The CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs could emit two kinds of fluorescence which originated from CDs and Eu-DPA MOFs, respectively under a single wavelength excitation at 275nm. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Eu-DPA MOFs decreased and the fluorescence of CDs kept constant to result in ratiometric fluorescent response toward Cu2+ when they were used as the recognition parts and the reference, respectively. The obtained CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs for the detection of Cu2+ showed a wide linear range (50nM–10μM) and a low detection limit (26.3nM) with high sensitivity, stability and selectivity. The work provides a good method to construct fluorescent sensors based on MOFs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic frameworks
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Cu2+
2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid

Sensing mechanism of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs fluorescence probe
Optimizing the conditions for Cu2+ detection
Cu2+ detection based on CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route for CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs and the mechanism for Cu2+ detection based on the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs.
TEM images of (A-C) CDs and (D-F) CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs nanocomposites.
FTIR spectra of CDs (curve a) and CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs (curve b) nanocomposites.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength (form 280nm to 470nm). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.5μgmL−1 CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs before (curve a) and after the addition of 20μM Cu2+ (curve b) under the excitation wavelength of 275nm.

Effects of pH (The reaction time is 3min and the reaction temperature is 30°C) (A), reaction temperature (The reaction time is 3min and the pH of solution is 7.0) (B) and reaction time (The reaction temperature is 30°C and the pH of solution is 7.0) (C) on the F425/F615 of 0.5μgmL−1 CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs. (D) The plot of F425/F615 of 0.5μgmL−1 CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs versus time in the presence of 20μM Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.5μgmL−1 CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs nanocomposites in the presence of Cu2+ with different concentration (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10μM). (B) Linear calibration plot of F425/F615 versus the concentration of Cu2+. The excitation wavelength is 275nm. The reaction time is 3min, the reaction temperature is 30°C and the pH of solution is 7.0.

F425/F615 of 0.5μgmL−1 CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs in the absence (red) and presence (blue) of 1.0mM Cu2+ and 1.0mM other cation ions. The reaction time is 3min, the reaction temperature is 30°C and the pH of solution is 7.0. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of the sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cu2+ with carbon dots chelated Eu-based metal-organic frameworks

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)@Eu-2(*), 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) MOFs was synthesized by encapsulating CDs into the Eu-DPA MOFs for the detection of Cu2+. The CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs exhibited the uniform ball-flower-like nanostructures and were very stable in aqueous solutions due to their nanoscaled size and the ball-flower-like nanostructures. The CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs exhibits the characteristic emission of CDs and Eu3+. The fluorescence of Eu-DPA MOFs decreases as the recognition parts and the fluorescence of CDs keep constant as a reference with the adding of Cu2+ to result in ratiometric fluorescent response toward Cu2+.

Here, a novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)@Eu-2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) metal organic frameworks (MOFs) was synthesized by encapsulating CDs into the Eu-DPA MOFs for the detection of Cu2+. The as-prepared CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs fluorescent probe was investigated by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results showed the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs exhibited the uniform ball-flower-like nanostructures and were very stable in aqueous solution due to their nanoscaled size and the ball-flower-like nanostructures. The CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs could emit two kinds of fluorescence which originated from CDs and Eu-DPA MOFs, respectively under a single wavelength excitation at 275nm. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Eu-DPA MOFs decreased and the fluorescence of CDs kept constant to result in ratiometric fluorescent response toward Cu2+ when they were used as the recognition parts and the reference, respectively. The obtained CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs for the detection of Cu2+ showed a wide linear range (50nM–10μM) and a low detection limit (26.3nM) with high sensitivity, stability and selectivity. The work provides a good method to construct fluorescent sensors based on MOFs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ is a necessary trace element in the organism, but the concentration of Cu2+ must be kept within a certain range in the organism [1,2]. Excessive Cu2+ will cause gastrointestinal disorders and kidney or liver damage, disrupt cellular metabolism, etc [3–6]. With the rapid increasing of the industrial wastewater and acid mine water, the Cu2+ pollution is throughout each aspect of modern life such as drinking water, food, various industrial products and is becoming more and more serious [7–11]. Although lots of methods for Cu2+ detection have been established, a simple and portable method to detect Cu2+ accurately and effectively is still very necessary for family routine tests.

Over the past few years, fluorescence sensors, especially the ratiometric fluorescence sensors, have attracted extensive interests due to their low-cost instrumentation, easy miniaturization for family routine tests and built-in correction [12–14]. Fluorescence sensors have exhibited the potential application in revealing trace amounts of analytes for portable family measurements [15]. The performance of fluorescence sensors strongly depends on the fluorescence probes [16–23]. At present, there are two kinds of fluorescence probes: organic fluorophores [17–19] and nanomaterial probes [20–23]. Although organic fluorescence probes have some unique advantages, they also have some inevitable disadvantages such as the tedious synthetic steps, poor solubility and stability, even biotoxicity, thus the application of organic fluorescent probes is limited greatly [24]. Nanomaterial fluorescent probes, especially the fluorescent probes based on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are attracting special attention because of their controllable pore sizes, strong encapsulation ability, large specific surface area, predictable nanostructures and hierarchical pores [25–27]. Some fluorescence sensors based on fluorescence MOFs probes have been proposed for various applications. For instance, strong fluorescence carbon dots (CDs)@MOFs was constructed for enhancing sensing [16]. Although fluorescence MOFs probes have achieved great successes in some applications, their large size and unstable structures would cause some problems in fluorescence detection, such as poor stability, low sensitivity, and so on [28]. Therefore, controlling the size and morphology of the fluorescence MOFs to prepare a stable and efficient fluorescent probes is very necessary.

In this work, a novel fluorescence CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs (DPA=2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid) probe was synthesized for Cu2+ detection. The resulted CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs exhibited the uniform ball-flower-like nanostructures and were very stable in aqueous solution due to their nanoscaled size and the ball-flower-like nanostructures. The porous nanostructures could also provide a large specific surface to increase the contact area between the probe and Cu2+, which improved the sensitivity of the sensor. The CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs showed two emission peaks by excitation at 275nm. The characteristic emission of CDs did not change after adding Cu2+ but the fluorescence of Eu-DPA MOFs would be quenched by Cu2+, thus a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ detection was established by using the characteristic emission of CDs as the reference and the characteristic emission of Eu3+ as the probe. Compared with ordinary fluorescence detection method, ratiometric fluorescent sensor can solve errors caused by the environmental factors, such as temperature, pH, etc. Accordingly, the probe showed low detection limit, good selectivity and high sensitivity and could be applied in real samples for family routine tests.

Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was obtained from Raven Rare Earth Metal Materials Co. Ltd. (Baotou, China). The 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) and chitosan were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, sodium chloride, potassium chloride and other reagents were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydroxyethyl piperazine acetic sulfuric acid (HEPES) was purchased from Shanghai Biological Engineering Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water purified by a Millipore-Q system (18.2MΩcm−1).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was operated by a Perkin-Elmer Spectrome 100 spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Company, USA) with KBr power. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on a FEI, Technai S-twin at acceleration voltage of 200KV. The measurement of excitation and emission spectra was performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The CDs was firstly prepared according to previous method [29]. Next, 44.6mg Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 25mg DPA were dissolved in 2mL ethanol and then the ethanol solution was added into 10mL CDs solution. The mixture was heated at 180°C for 72h to form CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs. The products were collected by centrifuging at 10000rpm for 10min and washing with ethanol and ultrapure water alternately. Finally, the as-prepared CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs were dried at 60°C for 24h. The preparation process was illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ by using CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The 10.0mg CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs was added into 100mL HEPES buffer (pH=7, 50mM) and then oscillated for 10min to obtain 0.1mgmL−1 CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs suspension. In fluorescence detection, 5μL CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs solution was used as a probe and added into 1mL HEPES buffer with different amounts of Cu2+. The above solution was oscillated for 3min to ensure the complete reaction between Cu2+ and CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs before detection. For the ion-selective experiments, the concentration of interfering ions and Cu2+ were 1.0mM. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained by exciting at 275nm.

The as-prepared CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs were firstly explored by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A–C, some spherical CDs with the diameter of 5nm were found. Their dispersion in a solution should be very good since these CDs dispersed on the substrate uniformly. The as-prepared CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs was much larger than CDs and their diameter were about 200–500nm (Fig. 1D–F). Black and white positions could be observed in the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs (Fig. 1E), indicating many pores in the uniform ball-flower-like nanostructures. Such porous ball-flower-like nanostructures might result in a large specific surface which was very important for enhancing the performance of fluorescence detection. Furthermore, the porous ball-flower-like nanostructures and the small size could also result in good stability and dispersion in a solution to improve the stability of fluorescence detection. As shown in Fig. 1F, these CDs did be wrapped in the Eu-DPA MOFs to form ball-flower-like CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs nanocomposites.

FTIR were also used to verify the formation of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs. A strong absorption peak at 3435cm−1 corresponding OH stretching vibration and an absorption peak at 1084cm−1 originating from CO stretching vibration indicated the existence of −OH on the surface of CDs (curve a, Fig. 2 ). Two broad absorption peaks at 3250cm−1 and 1632cm−1 were ascribed to the asymmetric stretching and deformational vibration of NH2, respectively. The results proved there were many NH2 and −OH on CDs surface and they could be used as sites for the formation of the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs. The characteristic absorption peaks of CDs were observed in the FTIR spectrum of the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs (curve b, Fig. 2), confirming the CDs was capsulated into the CDs@ Eu-DPA MOFs. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs, in addition to the above CDs absorption peaks, an absorption peak at 685cm−1 coming from pyridine and an absorption peak at 1718cm−1 belonging to CO appeared [30]. The peak at 2367cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CN of DPA was also observed. From the above analysis, it could be concluded that the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs were successfully synthesized. Besides, we also used Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S1, Supporting information) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. S2, Supporting information) to confirm the formation of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs. The crystalline structure was verified through X-ray diffraction (Fig. S3, Supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs fluorescence probe toward Cu2+ was shown in Scheme 1. The CDs showed tunable fluorescent emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity by exciting from 280nm to 470nm (Fig. 3 A). The fluorescence intensity increased and then gradually became weaker as the wavelength of the excitation increased. As shown in Fig. 3B, the obtained CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs fluorescence probe showed one strong emission peak at 425nm originated from CDs emission and three weak emission peaks at 592nm, 615nm and 694nm originated from the characteristic emission of Eu3+ under the excitation of 275nm. The latter three peaks were enhanced in the Eu-DPA MOFs because the coordination between the nitrogen atom of DPA and Eu3+ transferred energy from DPA to Eu3+ to sensitize Eu3+ luminescence [31]. After the addition of Cu2+, Cu2+ would replace Eu3+ to coordinate with DPA because it showed stronger coordination ability with the nitrogen atom of DPA than Eu3+. Since the fluorescence at 615nm originated from Eu3+, the fluorescence at 615nm was quenched by Cu2+ greatly. However, the CDs were a relatively inert material, even under high concentrations of Cu2+ conditions, and its fluorescence was not quenched. Based on the above analysis, we could use the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs at 425nm and 615nm (F425/F615) to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the conditions for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To gain low detection limit and high sensitivity, the conditions for the Cu2+ detection based on the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs were optimized. As far as we know, the sensitivity of the fluorescence sensor always depended on pH, concentration of probe, temperature and reaction time and they were optimized in the following experiments. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the F425/F615 almost did not change in the pH range of 5∼9 for 2μM Cu2+ detection. Then the effect of temperature on the fluorescence sensor was also explored. As shown in. Fig. 4B, the F425/F615 basically maintained constant for 2μM Cu2+ detection in the temperature range of 15–40°C. The good resistance capability of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs to environmental interference resulted from the built-in reference signal to correct the above factors. It could be seen from Fig. 4C that the response time for Cu2+ was within 1min. In the subsequent experiments, 5μL CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs solution was added into 1mL HEPES buffer with different amounts of Cu2+ and the solution was oscillated for 3min to ensure the complete reaction between Cu2+ and CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs. The rapid response might result from the porous nanostructures of the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs to improve the mass transfer. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs was very stable since the F425/F615 was nearly unchanged as it was used as probe to detect Cu2+ after one month (Fig. 4D). The good stability might result from its porous ball-flower-like nanostructures and the small size, which made the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs possess the potential as fluorescent biosensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ detection based on CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, 1.0mM Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Cd2+ were detected instead of 1.0mM Cu2+ in sequence to explore the selectivity of ratiometric fluorescent sensor. As shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S4 (Supporting information), these chemicals almost did not show interference on F425/F615. We also explored the interference from the Pb2+, Hg2+ and As3+ on the newly designed ratiometric fluorescent sensor (Fig. S4, Supporting information). Only little quenching effect came from Pb2+ and As3+ was observed. The results clearly indicated the good selectivity of the newly designed sensor for Cu2+ detection. The good selectivity might originate from the high selective coordination between Cu2+ and DPA.

The ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs was further used to detect Cu2+ in the water from Ganjiang River (a famous river in Jiangxi Province, China) for assessing the practicability. The water samples were firstly filtered using a dialysis tube (MWCO: 3500) before being spiked with Cu2+ at different concentrations. The added standard Cu2+ solution could be measured with good recoveries (Table S1, Supporting information). The results suggested that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs was accurate and credible. Therefore, the CDs@Eu-DPA sensor was capable of monitoring trace levels of Cu2+ in real water samples.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized porous CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection. The ratiometric fluorescent Cu2+ sensing probe could effectively overcome the interference from external environment, such as pH, temperature and other factors. The small size and the porous structure of CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs improved the stability and accuracy of Cu2+ sensing. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@Eu-DPA MOFs nanocomposites showed a rapid response, a low detection limit and a wide linear range with high sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+detection. And this method could be expected for Cu2+ detection in drinking water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21465014, 21465015 and 21665012), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20143ACB21016), The Ministry of Education by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20133604110002), the Ground Plan of Science and Technology Projects of Jiangxi Educational Committee (KJLD14023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.029.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Juan Hao received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2014 from Jiangxi Science and technology Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Fenfen Liu received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2013 from Jiangxi Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Nan Liu received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2016 from Jiangxi Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Mulan Zeng received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2014 from Jiangxi Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Yonghai Song received his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, China. He is currently working as a professor at Jiangxi Normal University. His current research interests focus on biosensors.

Li Wang received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, China. She is currently working as a professor at Jiangxi Normal University. Her current research interest is focused on biosensors.
